blocks:
Ayahs

سَبِّحِ ٱسْمَ رَبِّكَ ٱلْأَعْلَى (١)
Celebrate the Name of your Lord, the Most Exalted, (1)
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ فَسَوَّىٰ (٢)
who created and proportioned, (2)
وَٱلَّذِى قَدَّرَ فَهَدَىٰ (٣)
who determined and guided, (3)
وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَخْرَجَ ٱلْمَرْعَىٰ (٤)
who brought forth the pasture (4)
فَجَعَلَهُۥ غُثَآءً أَحْوَىٰ (٥)
and then turned it into a black scum. (5)
سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلَا تَنسَىٰٓ (٦)
We shall have you recite [the Quran], then you will not forget [any of it] (6)
إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ يَعْلَمُ ٱلْجَهْرَ وَمَا يَخْفَىٰ (٧)
except what Allah may wish. Indeed, He knows the open and what is hidden. (7)
وَنُيَسِّرُكَ لِلْيُسْرَىٰ (٨)
And We shall ease you into facility. (8)
فَذَكِّرْ إِن نَّفَعَتِ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ (٩)
So admonish, for admonition is indeed beneficial: (9)
سَيَذَّكَّرُ مَن يَخْشَىٰ (١٠)
he who fears [God] will take admonition, (10)
وَيَتَجَنَّبُهَا ٱلْأَشْقَى (١١)
and the most wretched will shun it (11)
ٱلَّذِى يَصْلَى ٱلنَّارَ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰ (١٢)
—he who will enter the Great Fire, (12)
ثُمَّ لَا يَمُوتُ فِيهَا وَلَا يَحْيَىٰ (١٣)
then he will neither live in it, nor die. (13)
قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَن تَزَكَّىٰ (١٤)
‘Felicitous is he who purifies himself, (14)
وَذَكَرَ ٱسْمَ رَبِّهِۦ فَصَلَّىٰ (١٥)
celebrates the Name of his Lord, and prays. (15)
بَلْ تُؤْثِرُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا (١٦)
But you prefer the life of this world, (16)
وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰٓ (١٧)
while the Hereafter is better and more lasting.’ (17)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَفِى ٱلصُّحُفِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ (١٨)
This is indeed in the former scriptures, (18)
صُحُفِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ (١٩)
the scriptures of Abraham and Moses. (19)

(Commentary)

The Surah commands the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — of the Exalted in a manner befitting His sacred presence. It enjoins the sanctification of His transcendent essence from mentioning any other name alongside His, or attributing to others what must be attributed solely to Him, such as creation (الخلق), governance (التدبير), and provision (الرزق). It also contains a promise to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله of support through knowledge and preservation, and of enabling him to follow the path that is easiest and most effortless for the delivery of the message (التبليغ) and most suitable for the call (الدعوة).

The context of the verses at the beginning of the Surah is Meccan. As for its conclusion—namely His saying: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ تَزَكَّى (Felicitous is he who purifies himself)—it has been narrated through the channels of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام, as well as through Sunni channels, that it refers to the zakat al-fitra (زكاة الفطرة) and the Eid prayer. It is well known that fasting and its subsequent zakat al-fitra and Eid prayer were only legislated in Medina after the Migration (الهجرة); thus, the concluding verses were revealed in Medina.

Therefore, the beginning of the Surah is Meccan and its end is Medinan. This does not contradict the reports stating that the Surah is Meccan, as such reports can be understood to refer to the opening of the Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: سَبِّحِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ الْأَعْلَى (Celebrate the Name of your Lord, the Most Exalted). This is a command to hallow His Name and sanctify it. Since the sanctification is linked to the "Name" (الاسم)—which is the outward word denoting the "Named" (المسمى)—and since the name only occurs in speech, its sanctification consists in not mentioning alongside it that which the Exalted is transcendent above. This includes mentioning deities, partners, or intercessors and attributing Lordship (الربوبية) to them, or mentioning acts exclusive to the Exalted—such as creation (الخلق), origination (الإيجاد), provision (الرزق), giving life (الإحياء), and causing death (الإماتة)—and attributing them to other than the Exalted. It also includes mentioning actions unbecoming of His sacred presence—such as incapacity (العجز), ignorance (الجهل), injustice (الظلم), and heedlessness (الغفلة), and similar attributes of deficiency (النقص) and blemish (الشين)—and attributing them to Him.

In short, the sanctification of His Name means stripping one's speech of any mention that is incompatible with the mention of His Name. This constitutes His sanctification at the level of speech, corresponding to His sanctification at the level of action.

This necessitates complete monotheism by negating manifest polytheism (الشرك الجلي), as in His saying: وَإِذا ذُكِرَ اللهُ وَحْدَهُ اشْمَأَزَّتْ قُلُوبُ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ وَإِذا ذُكِرَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِ إِذا هُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ (When Allah is mentioned alone, the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter shrink with aversion, but when those besides Him are mentioned, behold, they rejoice) [Az-Zumar 45], and His saying: وَإِذا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ وَحْدَهُ وَلَّوْا عَلى أَدْبارِهِمْ نُفُوراً (And when you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, they turn their backs in aversion) [Al-Isra 46].

The annexation of "Name" to "Lord" (الرب), and "Lord" to the second-person pronoun, supports what we have presented. The meaning is: "Sanctify the Name of your Lord whom you have taken as a Lord, and whom you call toward as the Lord and God." Thus, let there not occur in your speech, alongside the mention of His Name with Lordship, the mention of anyone else in a way that contradicts His being named with Lordship according to how He has made Himself known to you.

His saying: الْأَعْلَى (the Most Exalted) refers to Him who transcends every high being and subdues every thing. It is an adjective for "your Lord" (رَبِّكَ) rather than "the Name," and the ruling is justified by its meaning; i.e., "Sanctify His Name because He is the Most Exalted."

It has been said that the meaning of سَبِّحِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ الْأَعْلَى is to say: "Glory be to my Lord, the Most Exalted" (سبحان ربي الأعلى), as narrated from Ibn Abbas. It is also attributed to him that the meaning is "Pray."

It has also been said that "the Name" refers to "the Named" (المسمى), and the meaning is: "Sanctify Him, the Exalted, from all attributes and actions that do not befit His sacred presence."

It has further been said that the "Name" is mentioned while the "Named" is intended, to glorify the Named. This is supported by the verse of Labid: "Until the year [passes], then the name of Peace be upon you both." Thus, the meaning is: "Sanctify your Lord, the Most Exalted."

It has also been said that the meaning is to sanctify His Names from what is unbecoming, by not interpreting those that have been revealed without necessity, nor leaving them in their literal sense if the original linguistic meaning is impossible for the Exalted. Furthermore, one should not apply His exclusive names—like the Supreme Name Allah (اسم الجلالة)—to others, nor utter them in unsuitable places like the latrine, and so on.

The meaning we previously presented is broader, more comprehensive, and more suitable to the context of the subsequent verses: سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلا تَنْسى (We shall have you recite, then you will not forget) and وَنُيَسِّرُكَ لِلْيُسْرى فَذَكِّرْ (And We shall facilitate for you the easy way. So admonish...). The context is one of commissioning for reminder (التذكرة) and delivery of the message (التبليغ). Thus, He began first by rectifying the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله speech and stripping it of everything that suggests manifest or hidden polytheism by commanding the sanctification of his Lord's Name. Secondly, He promised to make him recite such that he would not forget anything of what was revealed to him, and to facilitate the method of delivery for him. Then, He commanded him to remind and deliver. Reflect on this.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى (who created and proportioned). To "create" (خلق) a thing is to gather its parts, and His "proportioning" (تسوية) it is to make them balanced such that each is placed in its appropriate position and given its due right, like placing each human limb in its suitable location.

Although "creation" and "proportioning" are mentioned in absolute terms, they encompass all created beings that possess composition (تركيب) or a semblance of composition.

This verse, along with the following three, describes the divine governance (التدبير الإلهي) and serves as a proof for His absolute Lordship (الربوبية).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى (and who determined and guided). That is, He made the things He created according to specific measures (مقادير) and defined limits in their essences (ذوات), attributes, and actions, which they do not exceed. He equipped them with what suits what He determined for them, thus guiding them to their destiny. Every being follows the path determined for it through a divine, ontological guidance (هداية ربانية تكوينية), just as the infant is guided to its mother's breast, the chick to the feeding of its parents, the male to the female, and the seeker of benefit to its benefit, and so forth.

The Exalted said: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ (There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except by a known measure) [Al-Hijr 21]. He also said: ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ (Then He made the way easy for him) [Abasa 20], and: لِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيها (Everyone has a direction to which he turns) [Al-Baqarah 148].

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِي أَخْرَجَ الْمَرْعى (and who brought forth the herbage). The "herbage" (المرعى) is what livestock graze upon; thus, Allah is the one who brought it forth, i.e., caused it to grow.

His saying, the Exalted: فَجَعَلَهُ غُثاءً أَحْوى (then turned it into blackened stubble). Stubble (الغثاء) is the dried grass and plants that a flood casts onto the side of a valley. What is meant here—as has been said—is dried vegetation, and "blackened" (الأحوى) means black.

The bringing forth of herbage to nourish animals and then turning it into blackened stubble are among the instances and evidences of Lordly governance (التدبير الربوبي), just as creation, proportioning, determination, and guidance are.

His saying, the Exalted: سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلا تَنْسى إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللهُ إِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ الْجَهْرَ وَما يَخْفى (We shall have you recite, then you will not forget, except what Allah wishes. Indeed He knows the manifest and what is hidden). It is stated in al-Mufradat: "Recitation (القراءة) is the joining of letters and words to one another in rhythmic chanting (الترتيل). This is not said for every gathering; one does not say 'I recited the people' when gathering them. This is evidenced by the fact that a single letter, when uttered, is not called a recitation." In al-Majma', it is said: "To have someone recite (الإقراء) is to take the recitation from the reciter by listening to correct errors; the reciter (القارئ) is the one who follows [the text]."

The Exalted's having His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recite the Quran is not like one of us having another recite by listening to what the reciter reads and correcting what he does not know well or errs in due to forgetting the revelation. It has never been known of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he recited something of the Quran poorly or erred in it due to forgetting the revelation, only to be corrected. Rather, what is meant is enabling him to recite the Quran exactly as it was revealed, without changing it through addition, omission, or distortion (تحريف) due to forgetfulness.

Thus, His saying: سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلا تَنْسى is a promise from Him to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that He will enable him to know the Quran and preserve it as it was revealed, such that forgetfulness is removed from him, and he recites it as it was sent down. This is the criterion for delivering the revelation (تبليغ الوحي) exactly as it was revealed to him.

His saying: إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللهُ (except what Allah wishes) is an exception intended to affirm that divine power remains absolute. This gift—namely, having him recite such that he does not forget—is not something that becomes independent of the Glorified such that He would no longer be able to make him forget. Rather, He remains in His absolute power; He may wish to make him forget whenever He wills, even if He does not actually wish it. This is similar to the exception in His saying: وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ سُعِدُوا فَفِي الْجَنَّةِ خالِدِينَ فِيها ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ (As for the felicitous, they will be in paradise, to remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty) [Hud 108], the explanation of which has already been provided.

The exception does not mean to exclude certain instances of forgetting from the general negation—meaning "We shall have you recite, then you will not forget anything except what Allah wishes you to forget." This is because every human being is in this state, remembering some things and forgetting others; there would be no point in specifying the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with this in a tone of divine favor (الامتنان) if it were common to him and others. Therefore, the correct view is what we have presented.

The verse, in its context, is not without support for what has been said: that when Gabriel descended with the revelation, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would recite it for fear of forgetting it. Gabriel would not finish the end of the revelation before the Prophet began reciting its beginning. When this verse was revealed, he never forgot anything thereafter.

It is plausible that this verse—سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلا تَنْسى—was revealed first, followed by His saying: لا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا جَمْعَهُ وَقُرْآنَهُ فَإِذا قَرَأْناهُ فَاتَّبِعْ قُرْآنَهُ ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا بيانهُ (Do not move your tongue with it to hasten it. Indeed, its collection and its recitation are Our charge. So when We have recited it, follow its recitation. Then its exposition is also Our charge) [Al-Qiyamah 16-19], and then His saying: وَلا تَعْجَلْ بِالْقُرْآنِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يُقْضى إِلَيْكَ وَحْيُهُ وَقُلْ رَبِّ زِدْنِي عِلْماً (And do not hasten with the Quran before its revelation is completed to you, and say, ‘My Lord! Increase me in knowledge’) [Taha 114].

His saying: إِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ الْجَهْرَ وَما يَخْفى (Indeed He knows the manifest and what is hidden). Manifestness (الجهر) is the complete appearance of a thing to the sense of sight, as in His saying: فَقالُوا أَرِنَا اللهَ جَهْرَةً (They said, ‘Show us Allah openly’) [An-Nisa 153], or to the sense of hearing, as in His saying: إِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ الْجَهْرَ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ (Indeed He knows the manifest of speech) [Al-Anbiya 110]. What is meant by "the manifest" is that which is apparent to perception, by the evidence of its contrast with His saying: وَما يَخْفى (and what is hidden), without restricting it to hearing or sight.

The sentence serves as a justification for His saying: سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلا تَنْسى. The meaning is: "We shall rectify your state in receiving the revelation and preserving it, because We know the outward and inward of all things. Thus, We know your outward and inward state, and the concern you have for the matter of revelation and your eagerness to obey what is commanded."

In His saying: إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللهُ إِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ etc., there is a transition (التفات) from the first-person plural ("We") to the third-person singular ("He"). The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to indicate the proof for the exception: the bestowal of knowledge and preservation upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله does not strip away the power to do otherwise, nor does it limit it for the Exalted, because He is Allah, the possessor of all attributes of perfection, including absolute power. Then the transition continues in His saying: إِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ for a similar reason.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنُيَسِّرُكَ لِلْيُسْرى (And We shall facilitate for you the easy way). The "easy way" (اليسرى)—the feminine of "easier" (أيسر)—is an adjective standing in place of its omitted noun, i.e., "the easy method." Facilitation (التيسير) means making easy. That is: "We shall make you such that you always adopt the easiest methods for the call and delivery, in word and deed; thus you guide some, establish the proof (الحجة) against others, and remain patient over their harm."

The outward expectation would have been to say: "And We shall facilitate for you the easy way" (ونيسر لك اليسرى), as He said: وَيَسِّرْ لِي أَمْرِي (and ease my task for me) [Taha 26]. The departure from this to His saying: وَنُيَسِّرُكَ لِلْيُسْرى is because the discourse concerns the equipping of the Prophet's noble soul itself and making it fit for the fulfillment of the message and the spreading of the call. This is in addition to the fact that "facilitating the easy way for you" might imply achieving what is already achieved.

Thus, the meaning is making him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله of a pure primordial nature (صافي الفطرة), naturally inclined toward choosing the easy path, which is the path of the primordial nature (الفطرة). The verse is similar in meaning to His saying, narrating from Moses: حَقِيقٌ عَلى أَنْ لا أَقُولَ عَلَى اللهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَّ (It is befitting that I should say nothing about Allah except the truth) [Al-A'raf 105].

His saying, the Exalted: فَذَكِّرْ إِنْ نَفَعَتِ الذِّكْرى (So admonish, if the admonition is beneficial). This is a derivation from what preceded: the command to sanctify his Lord's Name, the promise of having him recite the revelation such that he does not forget, and facilitating for him the easy way—these being the necessary conditions upon which the success of the religious call depends.

The meaning is: "Since the matter has been completed for you through your compliance with what We commanded, and Our having you recite so you do not forget, and facilitating for you the easy way, then remind, if the reminder is beneficial."

The command to remind was conditioned upon it being beneficial, and this is a real condition. If it were not beneficial, it would be futile (لغوا), and the Exalted is far above commanding futility. The reminder for one who fears (يخشى) for the first time produces an inclination in his soul toward the truth, which is its benefit. Likewise, reminder after reminder, as He said: سَيَذَّكَّرُ مَنْ يَخْشى (He who fears will take admonition). The reminder for the most wretched (الأشقى), who has no fear in his heart, for the first time produces the completion of the proof (تمام الحجة) against him, which is its benefit, and it necessitates his avoidance and turning away from the truth, as He said: وَيَتَجَنَّبُهَا الْأَشْقَى (and the most wretched will shun it). Reminder after reminder for him does not benefit at all; hence, the command was given to turn away from such a person. The Exalted said: فأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحياةَ الدُّنْيا (So turn away from him who turns away from Our remembrance and desires nothing but the life of the world) [An-Najm 29].

It has been said that the condition is formal rather than real, and it is merely an announcement that the reminder is inevitably beneficial in increasing obedience and refraining from disobedience, just as one might say, "Ask him, if asking is beneficial." For this reason, some said that "if" (إِنْ) in the verse means "certainly" (قد), and others said it means "since" (إذ).

This is refuted by the fact that the reminder is not always beneficial or effective, especially for one who stubbornly opposes the truth after the proof has been established against him. How could it be, when it has been said of them: سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ خَتَمَ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلى سَمْعِهِمْ وَعَلى أَبْصارِهِمْ غِشاوَةٌ (It is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not believe. Allah has set a seal on their hearts and on their hearing, and on their eyes is a covering) [Al-Baqarah 6-7].

It has also been said that there is an ellipsis (إيجاز بالحذف) in the speech, the implied meaning being: "So remind whether the reminder benefits or does not benefit." This is because he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was sent for reminding and providing excuse (الإعذار); thus, he must remind whether it benefits or not. The verse would then be like His saying: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ سَرابِيلَ تَقِيكُمُ الْحَرَّ (and He has made for you garments to protect you from the heat) [An-Nahl 81], meaning "and the cold."

This is refuted by the fact that the obligation of reminding even when no effect whatsoever follows is not established.

It has also been said that the condition is intended to suggest the unlikelihood of benefit in reminding those mentioned, as a way of mourning their state, as if it were said: "Do what you are commanded so that you may be rewarded, even if they do not benefit from it."

This is refuted by His saying immediately following it: سَيَذَّكَّرُ مَنْ يَخْشى (He who fears will take admonition).

His saying, the Exalted: سَيَذَّكَّرُ مَنْ يَخْشى (He who fears will take admonition). That is, he in whose heart there is something of the fear of Allah and the dread of His punishment will take admonition and be exhorted by the Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَتَجَنَّبُهَا الْأَشْقَى (and the most wretched will shun it). The pronoun refers to the "admonition" (الذكرى). By the evidence of contrast, "the most wretched" (الأشقى) refers to him in whose heart there is nothing of the fear of Allah. To "shun" (تجنب) a thing is to distance oneself from it. The meaning is: "And he who does not fear Allah will distance himself from the admonition."

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي يَصْلَى النَّارَ الْكُبْرى (who will enter the Great Fire). It is apparent that the "Great Fire" refers to the fire of Hell (جهنم), which is a great fire in comparison to the fire of this world. It has also been said that it refers to the lowest depths of Hell, which are the most severe in punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ لا يَمُوتُ فِيها وَلا يَحْيى (then he will neither die in it, nor live). The word "then" (ثُمَّ) indicates a sequence in the rank of the discourse. The negation of both death and life together means the eternal negation of salvation. Salvation—in the sense of the cessation of punishment—occurs through one of two things: either through death, so that the punishment ceases with the cessation of existence, or through the transformation of the quality of life from wretchedness to felicity and from punishment to comfort. Thus, "life" in the verse refers to the "good life" (الحياة الطيبة), similar to the saying regarding a dying person: "Neither alive to be hoped for, nor dead to be forgotten."

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ تَزَكَّى وَذَكَرَ اسْمَ رَبِّهِ فَصَلَّى (Felicitous is he who purifies himself, and celebrates the Name of his Lord, and prays). Purification (التزكي) is to cleanse oneself. What is meant is purification from the defilements of worldly attachments that divert one from the Hereafter, as evidenced by His subsequent saying: بَلْ تُؤْثِرُونَ الْحياةَ الدُّنْيا (But you prefer the life of this world) etc. Returning to Allah by turning toward Him is a purification from clinging to the earth, and spending in the way of Allah is a purification from the defilement of financial attachment. Even the ablution (وضوء) for prayer is a representation of purification from what the faces, hands, and feet have earned.

His saying: وَذَكَرَ اسْمَ رَبِّهِ فَصَلَّى (and celebrates the Name of his Lord, and prays). It is apparent that "remembrance" (الذكر) refers to verbal remembrance, and "prayer" (الصلاة) refers to the specific turning [toward Allah] legislated in Islam.

The two verses, according to their apparent meaning, are general. However, it has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام that they were revealed regarding the zakat al-fitra and the Eid prayer, and similarly through Sunni channels.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ تُؤْثِرُونَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا (But you prefer the life of this world). This is a transition (إضراب) in address to people in general, according to what their human nature calls them toward of complete attachment to the world and preoccupation with its cultivation. Preference (الإيثار) is choosing. It has been said that the address is to the disbelievers. In any case, the speech is intended as a reproach, and the transition to direct address is to emphasize it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقى (while the Hereafter is better and more lasting). The Hereafter is described as "more lasting" (أَبْقى) in relation to the world, even though it is eternal in itself, because the context is one of weighing the preference between the world and the Hereafter. It suffices for preference that the Hereafter is better and more lasting relative to the world, even if its eternal nature were set aside.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هذا لَفِي الصُّحُفِ الْأُولى صُحُفِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى (This is indeed in the former scriptures, the scriptures of Abraham and Moses). The word "this" (هذا) refers to what was explained from His saying: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ تَزَكَّى to the end of the four verses. It has also been said that "this" refers to the content of His saying: وَالْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقى.

It has been said that in the ambiguity of "the scriptures" (الصحف) and their description as "former" (الأولى) first, followed by their clarification and interpretation as "the scriptures of Abraham and Moses" second, there is an obvious aggrandizement of their status and a magnification of their importance.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Uqbah ibn Amir al-Juhani, who said: "When فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ (So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-Supreme) was revealed, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Place it in your bowing (ركوع).' And when سَبِّحِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ الْأَعْلَى (Celebrate the Name of your Lord, the Most Exalted) was revealed, he said: 'Place it in your prostration (سجود).'"

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ahmad, Abu Dawud, Ibn Majah, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh, from Uqbah, from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding سَبِّحِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ الْأَعْلَى, he said: "Say: Glory be to my Lord, the Most Exalted (سبحان ربي الأعلى)." Regarding الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى, he said: "He determined things with the first determination, then guided toward them whom He willed."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِي أَخْرَجَ الْمَرْعى, he said: "Meaning the plants." And regarding His saying: غُثاءً أَحْوى, he said: "It becomes dry stubble after reaching maturity and turns black."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to try to memorize the Quran for fear of forgetting it. So it was said to him: 'We have sufficed you of that,' and سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلا تَنْسى was revealed."

In al-Faqih: Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام was asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ تَزَكَّى. He said: "He who gives the fitra." He was asked about وَذَكَرَ اسْمَ رَبِّهِ فَصَلَّى. He said: "He went out to the open prayer ground (الجبانة) and prayed."

I say: This meaning was also narrated from Hammad, from Jarir, from Abu Basir and Zurarah, from him عليه‌السلام. Al-Qummi narrated it in his Tafsir as a disconnected (مرسلا) and anonymous (مضمرا) report.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to say: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ تَزَكَّى وَذَكَرَ اسْمَ رَبِّهِ فَصَلَّى, then he would distribute the fitra before going out to the prayer ground on the day of Fitr."

I say: It has also been narrated that the two verses were revealed regarding the zakat al-fitra and the Eid prayer through two channels from Abu Sa'id as a mawquf report, and similarly through two channels from Ibn Umar, one channel from Na'ilah ibn al-Asqa', two channels from Abu al-Aliyah, one channel from Ata, one channel from Muhammad ibn Sirin, and one channel from Ibrahim al-Nakha'i, as well as from Amr ibn Awf from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In al-Khisal, from Utbah ibn Amr al-Laythi, from Abu Dharr, in a tradition: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, is there anything in the world of what Allah revealed to you that was in the scriptures of Abraham and Moses?' He said: 'O Abu Dharr, read: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ تَزَكَّى وَذَكَرَ اسْمَ رَبِّهِ فَصَلَّى بَلْ تُؤْثِرُونَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقى إِنَّ هذا لَفِي الصُّحُفِ الْأُولى صُحُفِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى.'"

I say: The tradition supports that "this" (هذا) refers to the entirety of the four verses, as previously mentioned.

In al-Basa'ir, with its chain to Abu Basir, who said: "Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: 'We have the scriptures (الصحف) of which Allah said: صُحُفِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى.' I said: 'Are the scriptures the Tablets (الألواح)?' He said: 'Yes.'"

I say: It was also narrated through another channel from Abu Basir from him عليه‌السلام. It is apparent that the meaning of the scriptures being the Tablets is that they are the Torah, which is referred to in several places in the Quran as "the Tablets," such as His saying: وَكَتَبْنا لَهُ فِي الْأَلْواحِ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ (And We wrote for him in the Tablets of every thing) [Al-A'raf 145], His saying: وَأَلْقَى الْأَلْواحَ (and he threw down the Tablets) [Al-A'raf 150], and His saying: أَخَذَ الْأَلْواحَ (he took up the Tablets) [Al-A'raf 154].

In al-Majma', it is narrated from Abu Dharr that he said: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, how many were the prophets?' He said: 'One hundred and twenty-four thousand.' I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, how many were the messengers among them?' He said: 'Three hundred and thirteen, and the rest were prophets.' I said: 'Was Adam a prophet?' He said: 'Yes, Allah spoke to him and created him with His hand. O Abu Dharr, four of the prophets were Arabs: Hud, Salih, Shu'ayb, and your Prophet.' I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, how many books did Allah reveal?' He said: 'One hundred and four books. He revealed ten scriptures to Adam, fifty scriptures to Seth, thirty scriptures to Akhnukh—who is Idris, and he was the first to write with the pen—and ten scriptures to Abraham, and the Torah, the Gospel, the Psalms, and the Criterion (الفرقان).'"

I say: This was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn Asakir from Abu Dharr, except that he did not mention the scriptures of Adam, and he mentioned ten scriptures for Moses before the Torah.
